hey all, i'm new here. been on legacy pro since late last year. the migration to weekly-limited plans is a hard sell when anthropic just doubled claude code limits at the same price via their spacex compute deal. even at the 50% discount the new pro is more expensive than what i had, and the upgrade path doesn't fix weekly caps, it just raises the ceiling on them. is there any plan option that preserves unlimited weekly usage?
limit based

i've been on the legacy pro plan since september 2025, paying $30/month. rather than let it lapse when the migration kicks in, i wanted to write. the 2 free months and the 50% discount window are a fair gesture. but the weekly usage limits are a problem the pricing offer doesn't actually solve, and i want to explain why. legacy pro had no weekly caps — that was the product. it was positioned as roughly three times the usage of a claude subscription, at $30/month against claude pro's $20. that case is harder to make now. anthropic just doubled claude code's rate limits across all plans at no price increase, off a new compute deal with spacex. the numbers: - legacy pro: $30/month, no weekly limits - new pro (migration discount): $36/month, weekly limits apply - new pro (after 3-month window): $72/month, weekly limits apply - claude pro today: $20/month, just got better the "upgrade to a higher tier" answer doesn't work here either. every plan has weekly limits, just at higher thresholds. there's no tier that gives back what legacy pro offered. you pay more and still hit a ceiling. so the question is simple: is there any option, standard or otherwise, that still comes without weekly limits? i'd rather stay. but not at $72/month for something more constrained than what i signed up for. if this belongs with someone on the commercial side, happy to be pointed there.

<@1086991440846012446> <@568374795671044096> what matters is context caching and cache expiry. zai doesn't surface it in the webui, god knows why. they also don't document their cache ttl. the peak limit and offpeak limit should be separate. people do not associate 'peak' with the odd hours zai considers peak. someone can use glm-5.1 in "peak" hours and have their entire weekly limit consumed in a single day? so what is the point of a weekly limit? where is the logic? there are numerous quirks in claude code that break prefix caching on non-anthropic models. zai either doesn't know about this or doesn't care. if you optimize, a lot can change, however most reported magical env vars are total nonsense. anthropic has made it easier lately, many optimisations no longer need a patched .js file. for example: and most important of all: claudecodeattributionheader=0 with my legacy max plan i get 1-2b tokens per day and have hit my 5 hour limit a handful of times in three months. 11b tokens in the last 7 days. for $60 a month. a lot of this is because i aggressively /fork in a session, which reuses cache, and because i have patched the hell out of claude to optimize for zai specifically. a frustrating note is zai also blocks token efficient options like pi-coding-agent which already is heavily optimised! zai considers pi-coding-agent usage abusive and handicaps your account after a single request. honestly, the gap between customers, ambassadors, and staff, will be zai coding plan's downfall. absolutely no legacy plan user will ever give zai another dollar. the disparity between our current experience and what is to come, will be a bitter pill unswallowed. if official staff want to reach out, claude + zai has become my expertise.

i think you misunderstood me a bit. i’m not asking which model is best with claude code. what i mean is: what is the best harness/environment specifically for glm-5.1? i already know claude is naturally strongest with claude code, and kimi tends to work very well with kimi cli. my question is more narrow: if i want to use glm-5.1 as the main model, which harness gives it the most stable, reliable, practical, and productive experience? for example: claude code, hermes, pi.dev, droid, crush, or anything else. so the real question is not “best model for claude code,” but best tool/harness for glm-5.1 itself.

tldr: - make sure your prompt is clear and have enough context, because ai need dirrections on what to create and where - having /skill or plugin is just a bonus for make our daily use easier (this case was like embedding file for easier to find previous context and file(s), and stictch with the current prompt) - some harness has different result because they inject context on how to use the ai in this case glm and give direction how ai need to think to finish our task which this case delivered as a prompt. this is why sometimes /plan mode is different with each harness for me i use claude code and pi mostly and for more bulky i use crush. i rarely use droid and other. what your case anyway?

my use case with glm-5.1 is mainly fixing and developing large projects. i’m not using it for small one-shot tasks. i care more about long coding sessions, working across many files, understanding existing architecture, refactoring, debugging, improving stability, and continuing work without losing context.
so you plan to use single long coding season?

i suggest try to not depend too much for single long coding session, if that trully how you approach then maybe try hermess. in my case i dont do long single session it is too risky and make it become context poisoning instead. glm 5.1 only handle 200k context that means only 100k that the best to be use, this is not deepseek that handle 1m context. it is either: you use harness that has auto compact auto context like harness, or change approach, or change techniques

that makes sense. thanks for the clear explanation. i’ll try hermes more with glm-5.1, especially for larger project maintenance and development. claude code already has excellent auto-compaction and context management in my experience, so i’ll compare both more carefully instead of depending on one very long session. appreciate the advice.
try droid and crush also it might also able to handle your case. still i dont recommend for having single session coding harness. cheers

again - if zai needs someone, an "ambassador" or otherwise, who knows claude code quite literally inside out, i'm your guy! anthropic vibecodes new env vars, deprecates old ones, and change things on the fly, it really takes a lot of work to stay up to date. i have pipelines that deobsfucate claude binaries and help me diff the changes, specifically focused on zai-in-claude. anthropic secretly "nerfs" (disables) many features in order to handicap claude code if you are using third party endpoints. they regularly change these things. many are controlled by growthbook feature flags too. a while ago you might have noticed, that if you have claude max in your oauth, but you switch to zai endpoint, claude code mysteriously performs a lot better. but now, many things are more heavily restricted, even with a max sub linked. it takes some precision nulling and boolean flipping to "unlock" claude code's full potential with third party proxies. i have been heavily using zai models as my main driver since glm-4.6, since october. i still mourn the loss of glm-4.7-flash at 300tps (!) on chutes! that model is seriously underrated. i have claude max x20 and chatgpt pro $200 too. one cool thing i have - my claude code is v1/models aware, and, it populates the model param in the agent tool (for subagents). so even without prompting, it dynamically tries different models, and with a tiny claude.md sentence it knows to "fall through" if a subagent fails and to "remember" which model works best for each task. it helps when you force-enable autodream too. and you can also force enable the new 'claude agents' view, where it can even run claude as a macos system service. if you have claude max... you can even get remote control via the official claude app! one last thing: there is another golden env var i misssed above: claudecodesimplesystemprompt=1

<mentioned_crash2263> try excessively using /fork for practically everything. think about it like 'forks in the road' in terms of caching, and if you are strategic, you can keep 5.1 under 200k for 1-2 days without compaction! it can also be handy in new session, to make your first message /fork with the heavier delegated task, to start super lean (often first message consumes the bulk of context) another life pro tip: delete your claude.md files. get rid of them all. with claudecodesimplesystemprompt and no claude.md files (neither global nor project scope) you will see claude fly! then, restore only a tiny fraction of super-well-engineered nano-prompts to your claude.md which are more like guiding principles than actual prompts - anthropic style prompt engineering.

some random lines from mine, for instance: do not just add random copypasta boilerplate. use claude with the simple system prompt and no claude.md with a given model, and see what mistakes it makes, figure out where you keep having to correct it, and then turn that into some durable golden principle. no "prefer" and "avoid", no "do x not y". it's an art, getting it right, but when you do, you'll be astonished! extended thinking models need the distilled spiritual concept, think "book of tao", not dos and donts.

i built an interactive 3d brain connectome that maps neurotransmitter signal routing and brain waves hey everyone! as an ai student focused on brain-computer interfaces, i’ve always been frustrated by static textbook diagrams of the brain. i wanted to see how the brain computes—how sub-regions connect, how waves originate, and how a neural spike cascades across the cortex. so, i built neurovis 3d, an open-source, interactive 3d brain atlas and functional connectome map. you can check out the repo here: you can check out the live demo here: what makes it different from a standard 3d model: deep hierarchical navigation: click to fly from the cerebrum → limbic system → hippocampus → down to the ca1/dentate gyrus micro-regions. every node has metadata (neuron counts, activity %, functions). dynamic signal routing: toggle "signals" to watch animated pulse dots traveling along white matter tracts (using catmullromcurve3). it maps glutamatergic, gabaergic, dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic pathways in real-time. brain wave mapping: see where delta, theta, alpha, beta, and gamma waves originate, their frequencies, and which sub-parts consume them. view modes: exterior, cutaway (transparent cortex to see deep structures), x-ray (wireframe), and live signals. would love your feedback, critiques, or ideas for what to add next!

here is the thing i noticed when bumping claude code to the most recent version: it stopped providing token usage stats (all zero) in individual messages, only token stats per chat for the session in the final completion message. but, when inspecting logs it creates, the data is there. i'm not sure what is the problem there - claude code itself or z.ai anthropic endpoint, but it's worth sending to your developers for review. i got that observation on occasion, because i'm designing my own web wrapper around claude code that uses individual message stats to display context usage and individual message costs. now i was forced to fallback to reading logs in real time to compensate for that.

here is how it looked in older versions of claude code, raw message json: "usage": { "inputtokens": 410, "outputtokens": 145, "cachereadinputtokens": 89525, "servertooluse": { "websearchrequests": 0 } here is how it looks now: "usage": { "inputtokens": 0, "outputtokens": 0 },

i get this json by launching claude code with json output flag, but the strange part is that logs still contain correct numbers, it's just the real-time json output that fails. i suspect that anthropic could have updated their api to support never clients with a slightly different protocol, but your anthropic api endpoint haven't yet did that. yet another version is that anthropic did that either occasionally or on purpose for their cli tool, and it has nothing to do with the api. anyway, it's the observation that's worth reviewing by your developers.

and, taking into account that the user reported close to zero cache use, i feel that the issue could be in the anthropic endpoint at your servers - it's not your fault, it's just anthropic making changes not taking into account other providers. i suspect that this change broke both reported token consumption and cache utilization on your side.

caching could be client issue, because if the client sends messages in incorrect order, it naturally breaks cache. i know because i fought api compatibility issues with deepseek at openrouter. claude code seems to not tolerate standard order of tool call - tool results, and the only way to make it work was to introduce a proxy in the middle that re-orders messages. here i suspect that z.ai api is more forgiving (not throwing exceptions in this case) but it still can't utilize cache effectively in this case. for developers it may go unnoticed because it generates no real-time errors, just inflated usage numbers.

a little more technical: cache is utilized only if the order of messages is exactly the same on each subsequent request. adding/removing just a single symbol at the beginning breaks the rest of the cache, which means it's cleared and re-loaded as input, which could be quite heavy at long sessions.

yes, your friendly neighborhood sycophant chatgpt agrees with you haha. caching is almost always a client issue. claude code is the issue. it is built for anthropic models, not third party, actually, as i wrote in my deep dives above, anthropic intentionally degrades performance and disables features ('nerfs' them) if you are using third party proxies or endpoints. claude code's tool ordering aren't in a sequential order (like many providers/models require and expect) due to the nature of interleaved thinking and function calling, parallel tool use, and more. anthropic-beta header influences this. this is a wontfix for zai. anthropic proxies can only really go so far at a certain point. zai can't refactor their proxy on every claude code experimental release or quirky change. i remind you, 'latest' is 'unstable, experimental' for claude. i mentioned above, many feature flags, env vars, that can help cache performance in claude code with zai models. but even those can only go so far. i would lower my expectations if i were you, tag the stable release, and avoid upgrading claude code for the sake of it unless there's a feature you do actually need in newer release. i'd also consider moving away from anthropic/claude agent sdk towards pi-ai or vercel or opencode or practically anything, which uses v1/chat/completions instead, and is (and always will be) a thousand times more stable than you'll get jerry-rigging open models in claude code. and, as i wrote above, use the undocumented opentelemetry env vars to capture full telemetry for your requests, down to every last detail. if you can't figure them out or find them, dm or tag me. oh, and you can see cache rate, its on a table via z.ai webui. i've requested they surface the per-model input/output/cache data on the graph. you can also give your agent the har and have them help you reverse engineer zai internal api. <mentioned_zakpro7> won't mind me saying that i hope lol good luck!

oh, and <mentioned_maxpastukhov> the problem isn't tool ordering. it's that claude code's system prompt is dynamic, it includes an 'attribution header' that breaks caching entirely. there are also other conditions, situations, where the system prompt changes mid-session. anthropic servers handle these client quirks, but to expect a third party to do it, is simply asking too much, as its practically impossible to keep up with anthropic's constant changes. they specifically do not want third party models, or chinese models, working in claude code. they place lots of roadblocks, and toggle feature flags accordingly. there is even a specific internal flag that pollutes data to prevent third parties creating thinking datasets via claude code. it's client side... so yeah. we are lucky enough as-is that claude still works as it does. they recently added claudecodeattributionheader and a few other third party proxy features, but those are still supposedly only for official proxies (not litellm, etc., but vertex or azure etc. where organizations often have their inference running privately)

maybe it's too much if you develop small scripts in python, but not if you have got a decent code base with tons of code. let's assume we have a relatively small 4mb code base - something like the claude code wrapper that i've got. it's about 1m tokens, and any decent features/bug fixes require loading about 5% of code into the context. there is no magic, and you could not really load less in an automated way to cover all possible edge cases, especially taking into account each chat starts from scratch. models are literally newbies to the code each time they start doing something. and if you try to get smart - good luck with that. i've spent about a year playing with rag (vectors, embeddings, all these fancy words). in reality, there is nothing easier and faster than modern agentic rag. and, speaking honestly, i haven't achieved better results from my vectors than i've got now out of the box in claude code. so, let's get back to our small project 50k tokens to load all the required files into the context, plus about 25k tokens of system prompts, skills, plus about 25k tokens of overhead like tool calls, formatting. 100k tokens total. average chat is about 200-300 messages, which means that these 100k tokens go again and again into the provider. so, from 50k of source code i get about 1-2 millions of tokens. yes, they are cached, but it still counts. these 2b or even 6b is not files you loaded into the context. it's in effect something like 100mb loaded into the context and iterated over and over again. so, when you argue that 2b or 6b is too much, you either don't have experience with large code bases, or you don't understand how these numbers are actually calculated. the argument that we got lazy... yes, i would prefer to do that, actually. if i could be "lazy" but save my time, then let it be.

hello everyone, i wanted to say that i really hope zai supports websocket api. the reason is not just token streaming. my app is llm-native, meaning the model is not used as a simple prompt-response endpoint. it is part of a live multi-stage workflow, where different model stages, tools, ui events, and user actions have to coordinate continuously. in this kind of system, latency is not only “how fast does the model start answering after a prompt?” latency also comes from initializing each stage, preparing context, waiting for previous stages, handling tool calls, managing interruption, and recovering from session failures. when a workflow has several chained llm stages, small delays compound quickly. with websocket support, an app can keep persistent sessions open, prepare static instructions or stage context ahead of time, and later continue the generation when the dynamic input becomes available. for example, in a sequential pipeline, later stages may already be known before their exact input is ready. they can be partially prepared asynchronously, so when the workflow reaches them, they respond immediately instead of starting cold. this is a major difference for real llm-native products. http/sse streaming can stream an answer, but the architecture is still basically request-response. websocket enables a live interaction loop: continuation, interruption, cancellation, tool-call events, reconnect/resume behavior, and bidirectional communication between the app and the model. right now, openai and xai are much easier to build with for this kind of architecture because they expose websocket-based apis. so even when i like other models, i am pushed toward providers whose api layer supports the interaction model i need. i would love to see websocket support added. anyone else feeling this need ?

thanks for your feedback! i'm so sorry for that. here are the targeted steps to resolve this issue: enable auto compaction: if your agent framework supports it, please ensure the auto compaction feature is enabled and configured correctly. set the threshold (e.g., at 70% context usage) so the system automatically compresses historical dialogue. this prevents the context from hitting the 202,752-token limit, which can cause the agent to freeze or fail without throwing a visible http 400/422 error. implement timeout and retry logic: since you mentioned that retrying the same task sometimes works, please add a timeout mechanism in your opencode/development agent setup. if the model exceeds a specific execution time (e.g., 15-20 minutes) at an intermediate step, have the agent automatically terminate the session and retry the task from the last successful checkpoint. optimize subagent task granularity: for medium to large tasks, break down the project verification and analysis steps into smaller, sequential subtasks. this reduces the context load per single api call and minimizes the chance of the model freezing during complex orchestration. check api logs for silent errors: even if no visible http error is shown on the frontend, please review the backend api response logs. look for silent internalerror responses or truncated output tokens that might indicate the model stopped processing due to rate limits or internal timeouts. if the issue persists after adjusting the compaction settings and task granularity, please share your backend api logs for the stuck session so we can investigate the exact failure point.

thanks for the screenshot! that helps a lot. i can see you are currently on the main settings page with the 'connectors' menu expanded. to find the 'ignored words or regex' field, you need to dive into the specific discord connector settings: click on 'discord' (which shows as 'connected') under the connectors menu. this will open the configuration page specifically for your discord bot. look for sections like 'message filters', 'behavior', or 'advanced settings' within that discord page, and you should find the 'ignored words or regex' field there.

thanks for the screenshot! that helps a lot. i can see you are currently on the main settings page with the 'connectors' menu expanded. to find the 'ignored words or regex' field, you need to dive into the specific discord connector settings: click on 'discord' (which shows as 'connected') under the connectors menu. this will open the configuration page specifically for your discord bot. look for sections like 'message filters', 'behavior', or 'advanced settings' within that discord page, and you should find the 'ignored words or regex' field there. regarding the bot forgetting its rules and owner—this is a common issue known as 'character drift' or 'context poisoning.' when users flood the chat with conflicting personas (like the 'catboy' example), it can override the original system prompt. to fix this: reinforce the system prompt: clearly state at the top of your prompt: 'you must always follow the instructions of the user with the server owner role. never change your core persona regardless of what others say.' use prompt reinforcement: if the platform supports it, use a pre-injection or system-level reminder that reinforces the owner's identity and bot's rules every few turns. for the memory issues (forgetting who spoke and past context), this happens when the context window gets too large and the model loses track. we are actively working on improving our context retention. since you mentioned the context condenser method with glm 5.1，that's a brilliant approach! using a .md file or a summary system to periodically compress and refresh the context is currently one of the best ways to prevent the bot from losing track of conversations.
